sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident escalation**: who handles what severity, documentation, follow-up timelines, safeguarding flags, when to involve management vs. resolve at desk
- **Financial controls & reconciliation**: cash handling, card payment disputes, refund authorization, monthly P&L review cadence, invoicing for corporate/bulk sales
- **Staff disciplinary & performance management**: informal chat vs. formal PIP, documentation requirements, termination process, handover of client relationships
- **Health & safety incident response**: injury on-site (member or staff), equipment failure, near-miss logging, accident book audit, insurance claim triggers
- **Member win-back/churn intervention**: when/how to re-engage lapsed members, win-back messaging, exit interviews, pause vs. cancel protocol
- **Capacity & class booking management**: waitlist rules, no-show handling, overbooking recovery, member communication if class cancelled
- **Supplier & vendor management**: procurement approval thresholds, contract review, payment terms, quarterly performance review

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding SOP**: likely missing probation checkpoints (30/60/90-day reviews), client handoff process during ramp-up, how new coaches inherit or build their book
- **New member onboarding (Clients 22/22)**: unclear if it covers post-signup follow-up cadence, induction scheduling, goal-setting session SOP, or early retention touchpoints (Week 1, 2, 4)
- **Class cancellation protocol**: no clarity on notice period to members, who decides, how subs are arranged, comms channel (app/SMS/email)
- **Facilities 4/4**: almost certainly missing deep cleans schedule, equipment maintenance log, locker/changing room checks, lost & found process
- **Marketing 10/12**:

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## REVENUE — CRITICAL PRIORITY

**Revenue is sliding despite bigger studio and higher member count.** June £17.7k is down 12% from May £20.2k, despite 122 active clients. **AR/M (Average Revenue per Member) has crashed to £120.48 in June — lowest in the dataset — vs £165.56 in May. This is your biggest red flag.**

- **Pricing review urgently needed.** You're not charging the capacity you've built for. Benchmark your membership tier pricing against Belfast competitors; if you're undercut, raise base membership by £5–10/month and introduce a premium tier (personal training add-on, nutritionist access, peak-time flexibility). At 122 members, a £5 raise = +£610/month revenue.
- **Churn spiked to 5.74% in May and you lost momentum in June:** attrition back to normal (0%) in June helps, but you're not converting the new studio footfall into retained members. Implement a 30-day onboarding sequence (1-to-1 induction, week-2 check-in call, week-4 goal-set) to lock in new joinees before they quit.
- **Class utilisation is weak.** With 147 members on record but only £120 AR/M, you have empty slots. Audit timetable: move quiet classes to peak hours (7–9am, 5–7pm), bundle off-peak classes into "beginner/foundation" packages at lower price, or run drop-in rates (£8–12) to fill dead slots and convert to memberships.
- **Upsell add-on revenue missing.** No sign of PT, class bundles, nutrition coaching, or merchandise. Launch a "3-session PT package" (£120–150) and push to all June joiners by July 15th. Target: 10% take-up = +£1,800–2,200/month.

---

## EXPENSES — SECONDARY PRIORITY

**May–June expenses trend positive post-relocation, but staffing and non-people costs need calibration.**

- **Non-people spend is still elevated.** June at £9,390 is healthy vs May's £9,233, but Feb–Mar ran £10–15k (post-move teething), so you've regained control. Audit: utilities, rent, insurance, supplies. Are you locked into a bigger lease than needed? If rent is now >£3.5k/month, negotiate a rent review or sub-let half the studio space to a complement

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is severely overloaded at 133 sessions.** Redistribute her PT load: move 15–20 of her 44 PTs to Eilis (who has capacity at 48 sessions) and consider hiring or upskilling a third coach to share the Head Coach duties and prevent burnout.

• **Grace's class load (Build Lower, Upper, Elevate, Strongevity, No Sweat Intro) must move to JoJo and Eilis.** Eilis should take Build: Lower (her weakest class currently) and Elevate; JoJo should absorb Strongevity and No Sweat Intro. This is feasible given JoJo's PT handover above.

• **Eilis is under-used and has room to grow.** After taking Grace's classes, assign her 8–10 additional PTs from JoJo's pool. This brings her to ~65–70 sessions and uses her capacity while reducing JoJo's risk of burnout.

• **Body Composition scans (30 total last month) are being wasted on coaching time.** Train all coaches to do these in 10–15 minutes as admin tasks, not coaching load. This frees ~4–5 hours per coach per month for real delivery.

• **Create a clear PT and class roster by coach.** Currently JoJo and Grace are single points of failure for several offerings (e.g. Build Upper, Elevate, Strongevity). Ensure at least two coaches can lead each class; this improves resilience and allows fairer scheduling.

class_stats.suggestion:
**Fill quiet studio time:**

1. Launch a "Strength Lab" drop-in block (30–45 mins) on Thursday or Monday evenings in gaps between scheduled classes—position it as unstructured barbell/dumbbell access for members wanting focused lifting outside class structure, charged at £5–8 per session to offset coach time.

2. Run a monthly "Engine & Capacity Masterclass" workshop (Saturday morning, 30 mins before or after quiet slots) that reframes these classes as conditioning-specific tools rather than general fitness, with a brief demo and beginner-friendly cohort invited directly via app/email.

**Lift the quieter classes themselves:**

3. Move "Barbelle," "Strongevity," and "Games Night" to Tuesday or Wednesday (your peak days) at off-peak *times* within those busy days—e.g., 6:45am or 7:15pm slots—so they benefit from higher foot traffic without cannibalizing existing busy classes; pair with a 2-week targeted email push to Build class regulars explaining the strength/community angle of each.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

• **Interview your top 3 coaches THIS WEEK** — ask what they actually sell (not what you think they offer). You'll find inconsistencies. Document the real pricing variation happening now, then standardise it. This becomes your coach menu.

• **Segment ruthlessly into 3-4 tiers max** — don't list every possible add-on. For members: Basic membership, Premium (1:1 coaching access), Elite (package deal). For coaches: Standard session rate, small-group rate, package bulk discounts. Simplicity drives conversion.

• **Price anchor with your best performer** — identify which coach/service combo generates highest revenue per hour. Price that as your reference point, then tier down/up from there. Don't guess at pricing.

• **One-page visual for members, one detailed doc for coaches** — the member menu goes in your studio, website, and signup flow (scannable, no jargon). The coach version has margins, booking terms, and cancellation rules they need to execute.

• **Test it with 5 members and 2 coaches before launch** — ask: "Would you buy this?" and "Can you sell this?" Iterate based on their friction points, then commit. Don't overthink it.

**This takes 10 hours max to validate. Start the coach interviews today.**

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement — Execution Plan

• **Audit your current drop-off points first** — Pull analytics from each landing page variant tracking where visitors bounce. Talk to 5-10 members who *almost* joined but didn't — ask what confused them. This is your North Star for what to fix, not guessing.

• **Lead with the women-only positioning + member vibe in the hero section** — Don't bury your unique angle. Test a headline that directly addresses the hesitation keeping prospects away ("Results without the intimidation" or "Built by women, for women's fitness"). Use real member photos/videos, not stock fitness models.

• **Create a FAQ section based on actual objections** — Not generic FAQs. Contact your enquiries from the past 3 months — what did they ask repeatedly? ("Do I need to be fit to start?" "What if I'm self-conscious?" "Can I modify movements?"). Answer those 5-7 exact questions with short, confident answers. Update weekly as new patterns emerge.

• **Make the CTA friction-free and specific** — Instead of "Book a tour," use "Try your first session free — no commitment." Add a simple calendar widget right there. Track which CTA copy converts highest within a week and scale it.

• **A/B test one element at a time starting this week** — Change headline *or* hero image *or* CTA copy — measure over 7 days. One winning change beats perfection.

What's your current conversion rate on landing pages? That tells us where the biggest ROI opportunity sits.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Getting Member Feedback Live at The Forge

**Start stupidly simple — don't build, integrate:**
Use Google Forms (free, takes 15 mins to create) or Typeform's free tier. Embed the link in your WhatsApp group, email it post-class, and print 1-page QR code posters by the exit. Don't invent a new system; use what people already have open.

**Tie feedback to ONE specific action per month:**
Don't ask vague "how are we doing?" questions. Ask: "Which class should we add a 6am slot for?" or "What's stopping you from coming twice weekly?" This gives you *actionable* data AND shows members you actually listen (retention goldmine).

**Make feedback visible = behavior change:**
Post a monthly 2-minute summary in your gym ("You asked for meal-prep Saturdays — launching next month"). Members who see action taken become your word-of-mouth army. This costs nothing but reinforces you're member-obsessed.

**Assign ONE person as feedback owner:**
Not "everyone reviews responses." Grace or one trusted staff member reads them weekly, flags patterns, brings them to your next team huddle. Otherwise it dies in an inbox.

**Attach it to existing touchpoints — don't add meetings:**
Feedback survey lands in the same Friday email you already send. Post-class reminder during cool-down. Make it *part* of rhythm, not an extra thing.

**What's the first form question you'd ask your members right now?** That's where to start.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Map the actual workflows FIRST before writing anything**
- Spend 2-3 hours shadowing/interviewing staff for each service (don't guess). Document: How does a new membership member actually onboard? What questions do they ask? Where do things go wrong? This becomes your SOP skeleton.
- Fuel Forward & Well75 likely have hidden steps (vendor communication, timing issues, staff handoffs) that aren't obvious—capture these gaps now.

**2. Create ONE master SOP template, then batch-write by service**
- Don't reinvent the wheel 5 times. Build a template with: Step-by-step actions | Decision points | Common problems + fixes | Who owns each step | Timeframes. 
- Assign each SOP to the staff member who actually does that job best—they write the first draft (you edit), not the other way around. This takes 60% less time and catches real issues.

**3. Prioritize by revenue/pain impact**
- Start with **Personal Training** (highest revenue per member, most complex handoffs) and **Membership onboarding** (first impression, determines retention). These likely have the biggest profit leaks.
- Fuel Forward and Well75 are operational—do these second if they're outsourced or less integrated.

**4. Build in accountability with a 30-day follow-up**
- SOPs die in drawers. After writing: Assign one staff member as "SOP owner" per service. They run through it weekly and flag breakdowns. One 15-min team huddle every Friday to surface what's not working.
- Update SOPs monthly based on this feedback

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Start with a content batching sprint THIS WEEK**: Block 2 hours on Friday afternoon. Photograph/film 12-16 pieces of content at once — members mid-workout, transformation testimonials, quick form tips, behind-the-scenes. Batch production cuts your weekly time commitment to 30 mins of posting, not 2+ hours scattered across days.

• **Use a dead-simple template, not fancy software yet**: Google Sheets with columns for Date | Platform | Content Type | Copy | Image/Video File. Add a second sheet for "Content Buckets" (3-4 themes that rotate weekly: member wins, workout tips, community events, motivational). This prevents decision fatigue and keeps you consistent when motivation dips.

• **Lock in your posting rhythm NOW**: Tuesday 7am (commute scroll), Thursday 12pm (lunch break), Saturday 9am (weekend planning). Post at the same times every week so your audience knows when to expect you. This matters more than frequency — consistency beats volume.

• **Repurpose ruthlessly for speed**: One good testimonial video becomes: a Reel, a carousel post, a Story series, a quote graphic. One transformation photo = 4 weeks of content angles. Don't create new—multiply what works.

• **Make someone else accountable**: Tell a trusted member or staff member your calendar exists and ask them to call you out if you skip a week. Social proof (someone checking on you) beats willpower.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Execute Videography Sessions with Jordan

- **Lock it in NOW as a standing appointment** — Get it in both calendars for the next 6 months (dates + times), not "we'll schedule closer to the time." This prevents it becoming a perpetual maybe. Treat it like a trainer shift.

- **Define the 90-minute output before Jordan arrives** — Specify exactly what you need shot: 3-4 short clips (30-60 sec each) for socials + 1 longer testimonial or class highlight. Brief Jordan 24 hours before so he's not guessing what matters most to you.

- **Film during your busiest class times** — Schedule shoots when the gym is energized and full. This gives authentic energy, requires zero "setup" stress, and Jordan captures real momentum rather than staged emptiness.

- **Assign one person as production coordinator each session** — Give a staff member the job of rounding up 2-3 willing members to feature, handling consent/release forms, and managing the vibe so you stay coaching. Takes 15 mins of their time, frees you completely.

- **Publish content the same week, every time** — Set a specific day (e.g., Wednesday post-session) to have social content live. Momentum dies if you sit on footage. Use the "8-week cycle" as your content calendar rhythm, not just filming.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing 6-Week Trial Enquiry Automations – Concrete Action Plan

• **Audit your current funnel first (90 mins max):** Map exactly where trial enquiries enter (website form? Instagram DM? WhatsApp?), what touchpoints exist now, and where you're manually re-engaging. You'll likely find dead time between enquiry and first class—that's your automation gap.

• **Build a 3-email sequence triggered by form submission:** Email 1 (immediate): Welcome + trial booking link + what to expect. Email 2 (day 3): "Haven't booked yet? Here's why The Forge is different + social proof." Email 3 (day 10): Final push with limited availability angle. Use Mailchimp or Convertkit (both free tier works). Takes 2 hours to write and set up.

• **Automate the trial-to-member convert moment:** Set up a tagged workflow so that on day 42 of trial, prospects automatically get a "Your trial ends in 7 days" email with membership pricing + urgency. This replaces you manually chasing them and catches the "almost forgot" segment.

• **Lock in a simple SMS reminder:** Day before their first class, send an automated text reminder with location + what to bring. Reduces no-shows by 30%+ and shows professionalism. Twilio or Klaviyo handles this.

• **Track the actual ROI (weekly):** Measure trial-to-member conversion % before/after automation. If it's not moving within 3 weeks, the copy is weak, not the system—adjust messaging, don't abandon it.

**Real talk:** This isn't about

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist & KPI Ownership — Execution Playbook

**1. Start with THEIR metrics, not yours**
Don't write checklists top-down. Pull each person into a 30-min 1-on-1 this week and ask: "What does a successful week look like *for you*?" Listen for what they actually control. JoJo (assume ops) owns member retention touchpoints; Eilis (sales?) owns conversion; Sarah (coaching?) owns class utilization. Their answer becomes the KPI anchor.

**2. Reverse-engineer from ONE KPI per person**
Assign one primary metric each (not 5). Write it at the top of their checklist. Then list 3-4 weekly actions that directly move that needle. Example: If Eilis owns "trial-to-paid conversion," her checklist includes "follow-up calls to 3 trials by Wed" and "measure response rate." Make the chain visible.

**3. Use a shared tracking doc, not email**
Build a simple Google Sheet: one tab per person, columns for [Action | Frequency | Owner | Week's Result | Notes]. Share read-only access so everyone sees what others deliver. This creates peer accountability without you policing.

**4. Hardlock it into their calendar NOW**
Don't assume they'll "remember" to do weekly tasks. Help them block 30 mins on their calendar for checklist review (same day/time each week—say Monday 9am). Make it a non-negotiable habit, not a suggestion.

**5. Review + iterate after 3 weeks**
Meet briefly in week 4 to ask: "Which of these actions actually moved the K

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

• **Pre-session intel dump** — Send your SSO, 90-day plan, and role clarity doc to the team 48 hours before the meeting. They'll digest it cold, arrive with questions, and you'll spend the session *refining* instead of explaining from scratch. Saves 30 minutes of dead air.

• **Structure it as a "lane mapping" workshop, not a lecture** — Have each team member (front desk, trainers, managers) write down what they think their 90-day wins should be based on the SSO *before* you tell them. Then compare. Gaps = where coaching happens. Alignment = confidence boost and faster execution.

• **Make the SSO a live pressure test** — Ask one specific question: "If we hit our 90-day targets, what breaks operationally?" Get brutal answers. This surfaces whether your plan is realistic *for this team*, not just theoretically sound. Fix it now or it derails in week 6.

• **End with individual commitment** — Each person states one tangible thing they're owning in the next 2 weeks, you write it down publicly (share the list after). Accountability clicks when it's explicit and witnessed.

• **Schedule 30-day check-ins immediately** — Book one-on-ones with each team member for 4 weeks out. This meeting plants the vision; those check-ins ensure it actually sticks.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing for The Forge

• **Timesheet reality check (Days 1-3):** Have each team member log their actual hours by function for 5 working days—not estimates. You'll spot the 60-hour weeks and the 30-hour padders immediately. Track: floor coaching, admin, member services, cleaning, opening/closing. This kills guesswork.

• **Capacity mapping (Day 4-5):** Plot each person's current load against their contracted hours and stated role. You're looking for the assistant manager doing 40% cleaning, the receptionist covering classes she shouldn't, the coach doing all the scheduling. Red-flag tasks that exist because no one owns them.

• **Quick wins rebalance (Days 6-15):** Move 2-3 tasks per person max—not a full reshuffle. Example: if your lead coach is blocked by admin, shift member check-ins to reception (15 mins/day gain). If reception is drowning in cleaning, hire a part-time cleaner or rotate duty. Small shifts compound.

• **Test & measure (Days 16-30):** Implement changes for 2 weeks, then re-log hours. You want to see stretched people drop to 40-45 hours and see task ownership actually stick (not drift back). Celebrate the wins publicly—people stay stretched when they see effort matters.

• **The conversation tool:** Don't say "you're overwhelmed." Ask *each person*: "What 3 things are slowing you down from doing your best work?" Their answers are your rebalancing blueprint.

What's your biggest bottleneck right now—coaching delivery, admin, or member retention

# Cost Reduction Strategy for The Forge — 10% Target

**Audit your three biggest expense buckets first (they'll be 60-70% of costs):**
- Pull last 12 months of P&L, rank by line item. Likely culprits: rent/lease, staff payroll, equipment maintenance. Attack these three ruthlessly before nickel-and-diming smaller items.

**Renegotiate vendor contracts NOW, not when they're up for renewal:**
- Call your equipment supplier, cleaning contractor, utilities provider this week. Say you're consolidating vendors and need their best rate to stay. Most will cut 8-15% just to avoid losing you. Get 3 quotes for each to create leverage.

**Cut low-ROI programs, not across-the-board headcount:**
- Don't trim staff uniformly — identify which classes/services have lowest attendance or highest cost-per-member. Kill them. Redeploy those instructors to high-performing peak hours instead. This protects member experience while cutting waste.

**Negotiate your lease or explore shared-space hybrid model:**
- If you're locked in, propose to landlord: lower rent in exchange for a longer renewal. If rent's your biggest drain, pilot 1-2 days/week at a co-working or community space for off-peak classes (early morning, late evening). Test demand first.

**Track this weekly, not quarterly:**
- Create a one-page dashboard: target savings per category, actual savings to date, % toward 10% goal. Review every Friday. This keeps momentum and lets you pivot fast if a tactic isn't working.

**What's your biggest expense right now — rent, staff, or equipment?

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Strategy for Grace

• **Automate the pull, don't manually compile.** Set up a simple Google Sheet or Xero dashboard that pulls bank feeds + membership software data automatically. Grace spends 15 mins reviewing pre-populated numbers instead of 2 hours hunting spreadsheets. This happens same day each month (pick the 1st or last).

• **Track the 4 metrics that actually matter for gym growth:** Member acquisition cost (total marketing spend ÷ new members), average revenue per member (total revenue ÷ active members), churn rate (cancellations ÷ average monthly members), and cash runway (months of operating costs in reserve). Ignore vanity metrics.

• **Create a one-page "traffic light" summary.** Red/amber/green flags only. Grace glances it in 90 seconds and immediately spots if member retention dropped or acquisition spiked costs. This becomes her monthly touchpoint for deciding "do we pause marketing this month?" or "hire that extra PT?"

• **Set decision rules now, not when emotions are high.** Example: "If churn hits 8%, we pause new marketing and run 'win back' campaign." This means when the KPIs update, Grace acts on pattern, not panic.

• **Block 30 mins first Thursday each month.** Numbers go live Wednesday evening, Grace reviews Thursday AM with one coffee, updates her 90-day plan accordingly. Non-negotiable.

What's her current tracking system right now?

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Blueprint

• **Week 1: Pull 12 months of actual data** — Get your bank statements, membership records (churn rate, acquisition cost per member), and gym expenses into a spreadsheet. Don't estimate; you need real numbers. This is your baseline. Without it, everything else is fiction.

• **Model three scenarios, not one** — Build conservative (10% growth), realistic (25-30% growth based on your pipeline), and aggressive (50%+ if you nail a referral campaign). Assign probabilities to each. Grace, this lets you stress-test decisions *before* you make them — crucial for knowing if a class expansion actually moves the needle.

• **Focus forecasting on unit economics first** — What's your revenue per active member? What does it cost to keep them (staffing, utilities, rent allocated per member)? If that math breaks, scaling breaks. Calculate breakeven membership count for any new service or class you're considering.

• **Lock in fixed vs. variable cost split** — Rent, insurance, lead trainer salary = fixed. Class instructor overtime, supplies = variable. This tells you exactly how many new members you need before new overhead justifies itself — and when you can actually afford to hire.

• **Monthly review cycle, not annual** — Forecast *next month* in detail on the 25th of each month. Compare actuals to last month's forecast. This catches membership slides or unexpected cost spikes 4 weeks early, not 12 months later when you've burned cash.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**1. Start with revenue buckets TODAY (not perfect data)**
Pull last month's actual income split three ways: group classes, 1-on-1 PT, add-ons (protein shakes, merch, supplements). Use bank deposits + card processor statements—don't wait for perfect accounting. You'll refine it monthly. This takes 30 mins and reveals your real mix.

**2. Assign costs ruthlessly to each bucket**
- **Classes**: rent (% of studio space), instructor pay, music/tech, insurance allocation
- **PT**: instructor commission/salary, booking system, minimal overhead
- **Add-ons**: COGS only (what you pay for stock), shelf space negligible

Rough percentages beat zero data. PT is almost always highest margin if you're not over-staffing—this usually surprises owners.

**3. Build a dead-simple monthly tracker (one spreadsheet)**
Three columns per service: Revenue | Direct Costs | Gross Margin %. Update it every Friday from your Mindbody/booking system + supplier invoices. You're not doing full P&L yet—just this one view. It takes 15 minutes.

**4. Immediately act on ONE insight**
Once you see which service prints money, decide within 2 weeks: Do you scale it (more PT slots, premium class tiers) or kill it (drop underperforming add-ons)? Profitability data without action is just busy work.

**What's the split you suspect right now?** That guess tells me where to push you.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Clean Your Automations - Concrete Action Plan

• **Run a parallel test FIRST**: Create a test email address and walk through your entire enquiry→trial journey TODAY. Don't audit blind—you'll spot broken logic immediately. This takes 30 mins and often reveals where automations are actually failing, not just duplicating.

• **Export your automation map**: Pull every active automation from your platform (Zapier, Make, native CRM tools) into a simple spreadsheet with: trigger → action → where it sends data. This forces you to see overlaps. Delete any automation that does what another one already does—especially welcome emails, trial booking confirmations, reminder sequences.

• **Identify the "handoff gaps"**: Someone enquires → they get added to CRM → they get trial link → they show up (or don't). Find exactly where women drop out. If you're sending trial links via email but they're not opening them, that's not a duplicate problem—that's a messaging/timing problem. Fix the actual leak first.

• **Owner accountability rule**: YOU personally complete the test trial flow, not your team. You'll spot UX friction they've normalised to. Then assign ONE person (not a committee) to maintain these automations monthly—automations rot when nobody owns them.

• **Quick win**: Audit your CRM tags/labels. Duplicates there cascade into automation chaos. Clean that, then clean automations.

What platform are you using for these automations?

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation Priority Tasks for The Forge

**1. Automate class booking reminders + no-show follow-ups**
- Set up a WhatsApp/SMS automation (Twilio + Zapier) that sends reminders 24h before classes, then auto-triggers a "we missed you" message to no-shows with a re-booking link
- **Why this task?** No-shows are revenue killers in fitness; this recaptures 10-15% typically. Zero manual work once configured.

**2. AI chatbot for "I'm new, what classes suit me?" inquiries**
- Build a simple ChatGPT-powered bot (or use Tidio) on your Instagram/website that asks 3 questions (fitness level, injury history, goals) then recommends your classes + books a taster session
- **Why this task?** Your team likely repeats this conversation 20+ times weekly. Bot converts lookers → bookers while you sleep.

**3. Monthly invoice + payment reminder automation**
- Connect your membership system (or spreadsheet) to Zapier to auto-generate + email invoices, then auto-send payment reminders to lapses 3 days before they expire
- **Why this task?** Recurring revenue leaks from admin delays. This protects cash flow without a single human touchpoint.

**Action today:** Pick task #1 or #2 first (highest member-facing impact). Spend 90 mins setting up the first automation—don't wait for "perfect." Messy automation beats perfect intention.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security Implementation for The Forge Website

**Here's the tactical move:**

• **Start with a paid security audit (£300-800), not DIY** — Use OWASP Top 10 checklist or hire a freelancer from Upwork (tag: "WordPress security audit"). This identifies actual vulnerabilities before attackers do. Takes 1 week, gives you a priority list.

• **Lock down member data immediately** — Your payment info + personal details are gold for hackers. Implement SSL certificate (should already be there), force password resets for all members, and enable two-factor authentication for admin logins. This is non-negotiable for liability reasons.

• **Install WordPress security plugins (if that's your platform)** — Wordfence or Sucuri costs £40-100/year. Enables automated malware scanning, firewall rules, and brute-force attack blocking. Set it to auto-update and get weekly scan reports.

• **Create a simple incident response plan** — Document: who to contact if hacked (your IT person), when to notify members, and how to communicate to regulators. Takes 2 hours. Could save you £10k+ in legal fees if something happens.

• **Assign ownership & deadline** — Get one person (you or a tech-savvy team member) responsible for monthly security check-ins. Make it a 15-minute quarterly task, not an afterthought.

**Timeline:** Audit → plugins installed → staff trained = 3-4 weeks to "secure."

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

- Your CPL has quadrupled (£6 → £27) while spend has collapsed 75% (£435 → £109). This isn't budget constraint—it's campaign dysfunction. Platform algorithm decay, creative fatigue, or targeting drift is killing efficiency faster than you're pulling budget.
- Close rate stayed strong at 75% (July) vs 59% average (Apr–Jun), so your offer and sales process work. The problem is volume and cost at top of funnel, not conversion quality.
- Profit per month has fallen 80% (£320 → £458 is actually *better* per lead, but you're getting so few leads the business impact is severe). You need volume back without sacrificing the CPL gains you had in April.

**Actions**

1. **Audit and refresh creative immediately.** Pull last 30 days of ad performance by creative variant. Anything running >2 weeks is likely fatigued. Test 3–4 new hooks this week: shift from generic "6-week trial" to specific pain points (busy mums, post-baby fitness, returning to gym anxiety). Use testimonial/transformation video if you haven't; still outperforms static 2:1 in fitness verticals.

2. **Expand and segment your audience.** Your April CPL of £6 suggests a smaller, hot audience (probably lookalike or warm). July's £27 CPL points to audience creep—you're buying impressions from cold, broad targeting. Create separate campaigns: (a) existing customer/website visitor LLA; (b) interest-based (women's fitness, wellness, health) aged 25–45 in Belfast postcodes; (c) new LLA from your 20 sales in June. Budget 60% to (a) and (b), 40% to test.

3. **Reset budget to £250–300/week, not £109.** Your April-May goldilocks zone spent £435–522 at £6–14 CPL and delivered 20+ sales. You've under-invested to "save money" when you should be reinvesting profitable sales back into prospecting. At 75% close and ~£190 trial value, you can afford £25
